title: Groundnut-Coriander Chutney
description: A fresh coriander and groundnut-based chutney with no garlic and mild ginger, perfect for tatte idlis. Versatile with optional tempering for added depth.

subSteps: If groundnuts are not already roasted, dry roast them in a pan over medium heat.
Roast until lightly golden, about 3-4 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Cool slightly before grinding.
VALIDATION: Groundnuts should be light golden in color, aromatic, and cool enough to handle.
Wash coriander leaves thoroughly with tender stems.
Slit 3-4 green chilies lengthwise, remove seeds if you prefer less heat.
Peel and measure 1/2 inch ginger piece.
VALIDATION: Coriander leaves should be clean and damp, chilies slit, ginger peeled and ready to blend.
Add roasted groundnuts, fried chana dal, coriander leaves, tamarind, green chilies, ginger, salt, and jaggery to a mixer or food processor.
Add 1/2 cup water and blend until smooth.
Gradually add remaining water (1/2 cup) while grinding to reach desired consistency.
VALIDATION: Chutney should be smooth, slightly thick, and fall slowly from a spoon. Consistency should coat idli rather than run across the plate.
Heat 1 tsp oil in a small pan over medium heat.
Once hot, add 1/2 tsp mustard seeds and cover with a lid.
Let seeds splutter for 15-20 seconds until they stop crackling.
Add curry leaves and a pinch of hing.
VALIDATION: Seeds should splutter vigorously and turn brown (not black). Curry leaves should be fragrant and slightly curled.
Transfer the ground chutney to a serving bowl.
Pour the hot tempering over the chutney.
Stir gently to incorporate the tempering flavors.
VALIDATION: Chutney should have a glossy finish with aromatic tempering oil visible.

seo.metaTitle: Groundnut-Coriander Chutney Recipe | Tatte Idli Chutney
seo.metaDescription: Fresh coriander and groundnut chutney without garlic, with mild ginger. Perfect for tatte idlis with optional tempering. South Indian chutney recipe.
